Summary Document

Overview

This document provides a concise summary of the infrastructure and services related to the Letters v2 project, focusing on data management, APIs, and related technologies.

Data Management

The data management infrastructure includes various SQL Server databases such as EligibilityMS, ProviderMS, DrugsMS, and LoggingDB. These databases support different aspects of the project, including eligibility, provider information, and drug data management. Additionally, there are multiple Blob Storage solutions for storing inbound faxes and Matis documents, along with other storage needs.

APIs and Services

Several APIs and services are integral to the system's functionality:

  1. Eligibility API: Manages eligibility-related queries and data processing.
  2. Provider API: Handles provider-related information and interactions.
  3. Drugs API: Facilitates drug data management and retrieval.
  4. Admin Portal API: Provides administrative functionalities and access control.
  5. Mail Service (Kaleidoscope): Manages email communications.
  6. IVR (Voice Recognition) API: Supports voice recognition capabilities.
  7. Turnaround Time (TaT) API: Monitors and reports on processing times.

Data Processing and ETL

The system employs various ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes to manage data flow and transformation:

  1. Azure Data Factory ETL and Databricks ETL: Facilitate data integration and processing.
  2. Salesforce ETL: Integrates Salesforce data into the system.
  3. Synapse ETL and SSIS ETL: Support data warehousing and integration tasks.

Additional Services

  1. EventProcessor Windows Service: Manages event-driven processes.
  2. MessageBridge Windows Service: Facilitates message routing and processing.
  3. Fax Service API: Handles fax communications.
  4. Document API: Manages document-related operations.

Security and Authentication

The system uses Okta for authentication and identity management, ensuring secure access to various services and APIs.

Communication and Integration

  1. Twilio: Provides communication capabilities, including SMS and voice services.
  2. Nuance: Supports advanced voice recognition and processing.

Monitoring and Reporting

  1. PowerBI: Used for data visualization and reporting.
  2. JobMonitor API: Tracks and reports on job statuses and performance metrics.
    This summary encapsulates the key components and services of the Letters v2 infrastructure, highlighting the integration of data management, APIs, and various supporting technologies.

data.content: Platform Architecture Map -- Recommended future state for platform architecture, focused on current areas of friction and paths to addressing them.

High Level Technology Roadmap -- Prioritized list of areas where engineering and technology investment should be focused over the next 12-18 months in order to modernize the platform, address tech debt and must-fix issues, and maximize near- to medium-term return on investment.
Cost Heat Map -- A representation of engineering products within the roadmap that allows straightforward analysis of relative cost and relative value.
Architecture Evaluation -- Analysis of system architecture, data models, scalability, flexibility, resilience, and alignment with growth projections.
Technology Stack Review -- Evaluation of technologies used (including development tools), their suitability, potential licensing costs, and the risk of obsolescence.
Scalability -- Evaluation of current architecture scalability as OncoHealth adds transaction volume, distinct customers, and staff.
Security Assessment -- Review of security measures, data protection, encryption and vulnerability management, primarily through discussion and review of documentation.

Plan for Simplified Technical Onboarding -- How to speed up the technical aspects of new client onboarding by creating configurability, building out-of-the-box compliance tools including audit trails, and enabling multi-tenancy.
Analytics Platform -- Recommended data and analytics tech stack appropriate for their volumes and reporting needs. Recommendation will be comprehensive, leaving room to scale but not overbuilding.
AI Adoption Recommendations -- Input where and how AI could be adopted into OncoHealth’s platform to enhance technology efficiency.
Code Review -- Assessment of code quality, maintainability, security practices, testing coverage, and adherence to standards, including quantitative team-level efficiency.

Team Efficiency & Productivity Analysis -- Quantitative readout on current capacity and output of OncoHealth’s current technology teams, based on proprietary analytics tools that Newfire has been developing since 2018.

Awareness - Developer and Product Management Expertise -- OncoHealth has requested that the assessment not prioritize evaluation of talent and team structure. However, if during the course of our work we observe any critical gaps or risks related to engineering or product management capabilities that may impact our assessment and recommendations, we will bring them to your attention.
Development Lifecycle -- Examination of software development methodologies, processes, version control, and deployment practices, including developer output.

Neal's notes:

  1. look at marten (C# library around events, possibly for writing the state machine)
  2. Preferred Regimens project (precursor to Admin Portal, predates SmartUM) is similar to the concept for regimens
  3. look at ElasticSearch for searching through regimens and providers, but not as a system of record
  4. Letters v2 is using nextjs for its UI, was intended as a PoC for matis improvements
  5. For UI, flutter was not the best choice partly because of staffing difficulties and also QA automation
  6. staffing with seniors: probably timeline is ok, mixed staffing: 12-18 months
  7. might be able to save TaT logic for later in project

Sean's notes:

  1. migration pipeline sounds difficult
  2. configurable case workflow sounds familiar

Jason's notes:

  1. scariest details likely in the drug checker
  2. Matis logic ~40%
  3. SmartUM logic ~60%
  4. drug checker needs rules per client/patient
  5. likes the idea of a using the data model to represent relationships between groupings
  6. users can be internal, providers, payer reps
  7. split global user table up
  8. gets rid of contrived user groups
Extension of SOW #1 - Details to be finalzied
Data is transferred to clients through a mix of push and pull mechanisms
source data quality gaps magnify work and risk
Enhancement: Data Activation Solution for creating managed data exports to clients
Enhancement: Standardized Gold Layer, Data Catalog, and AI Metric Steward to make reporting easier and more consistent
Enhancement: Operational Metrics for Data Product Manufacturing
Enhancement: Standardizing on Delta Live Tables and metadata driven transformations to lower costs of developing data pipelines and ongoing maintenance
Enhancement: End to end data lineage to support proactive knowledge production graph administration for faster problem resolution
Enhancement: End to end data observability for outlier alerting and monitoring to maintain SLAs
Enhancement: Comprehensive single upstream data source to lower implementation costs
Enhancement: Data contracts where DNA is seen as 1st class customer to improve product quality

data.title: New Provider Data Model - High Level Design.pdf

data.content:
Onco Extension - Goals DRAFT v32
Scope: 5 Weeks, October 13, 2025 - November 14, 2025
Additional SOW Goals:
● Create alignment on OncoHealth technical and leadership team around core
assumptions from the initial assessment, through an in-person workshop and additional
documentation.
● Reduce scoping risk for NewUM, validating the idea that there are between 30-80 main
points of configurability required for customers and identifying and characterizing “show
stopper” risks..
● Support the thesis that client migrations to NewUM can be accomplished with minimal
customer impact and support, enabling a rapid transition to NewUM once the necessary
capabilities are in place.
● Validate the reusability of three critical components, and evaluate necessary changes for
NewUM.
○ Drug Schema from AdminDB
○ Provider Schema
○ Eligibility file processing pipeline
● Deliverable: High level budget proposal for NewUM build project, updated as necessary
during the course of the engagement
● Deliverable: “Granularity” document that identifies a set of “configuration dimensions”
for NewUM (for example, being able to configure features based on member eligibility at
the payor level, the plan level, or the employer group level)
● Deliverable: Set of specific user experience requirements from operations teams,
including results of virtual workshop with Newfire UX team
● Deliverable: Validation of migration feasibility, focusing on challenges with moving
coarse grained data (for example: OADB drugs to finer-grained Admin Portal Drug
Standard)
○ Assess across intake migration, historical case data migration, and reporting
migration

● Deliverable: A team and build proposal for NewUM
○ High-level architecture and component diagram
○ Team roles and capabilities
○ Integration points with current OncoHealth Team
○ User experience strategy
○ Development methodology
● Deliverable: Catalog of configuration points identified from analysis of Change
Requests, along with sample configuration points identified during the assessment.
● Deliverable: Propose SmartUM migration and risk mitigation strategy.

The work to drive these deliverables will include:

● Run a review and discussion session from the SOW1 prepared documents
● Analyze CRs for each client, along with test plans, implementation plans, etc.
○ Focusing on whether a random sample of CRs will create migration challenges
(due to nonstandard use of data fields, information modeling issues, workflow
etc).
○ Generate example configuration points for the NewUM platform
● Review ZenDesk Production Support tickets to supplement analysis of Change
Requests from ADO
● Diligence on the data pipeline strategy
○ Understand the impact of custom data on the plan (data used differently per
client, overloaded custom fields, etc).
○ Emphasis on ensuring that current data intake processes can be adopted by
NewUM to minimize/eliminate client involvement in migrations.

● Interview and review Testing in Matis/SmartUM
○ Define high level testing plan and considerations for NewUM
○ Generate additional data points for planning configurable capabilities
● Additional review of other non-Matis services to identify additional strategic projects that
will support migrations and increase development velocity for NewUM.
● Workshop with CRS and clinical team and Newfire UX to identify team goals and key
requirements for updated NewUM interface and workflows, including an exploration of
requirements around case states and a move to a more episodic approach to prior
authorization approvals.
● Validate that the data team would be able to deliver the majority of their compliance and
reporting based on a unified data feed from NewUM to DataBricks (with emphasis on
identifying hard-coded reporting patterns that are tightly linked to Matis source data).
● Investigate variability of eligibility data, focusing on identifying configuration points.
● One or more in-person meetings to review go-forward plans.

item from Slack (11/7): Historical Case Data 

Case State topic (Cliff)

• Provider submit via portal → TAT starts.
• Fax / phone request received → TAT starts at receipt time.
• Case record created and queued for intake validation.

• Member found on eligibility → Yes.
• Ordering provider active → Yes (added if missing).
• Mandatory fields complete.
• Diagnosis in scope for delegated services → Yes.
• Regimen created, documents attached, billing type captured.
• Network / Benefit Check (Basic Admin Denial logic):
  – Servicing provider In Network → proceed.
  – OON provider + OON benefits → proceed.
  – OON provider + no OON benefits → Admin Denial (Row 6).
• Scope Check:
  – Includes at least one PA Required (In Scope) drug/code.
  – Any PA Exempt or OOS codes identified are tagged for separate notification handling.

• Regimen for review assigned to clinical team.
• Reviewer validates request information and attached documentation for completeness.
• “All information received?” → No → initiate outreach (NMI / CCF / P2P).
• Up to 3 outreach attempts logged.
• “Change From / Change To” requests handled within state.
• Mixed-PA Logic:
  – PA Required (In Scope): reviewed clinically.
  – PA Exempt: labeled informational, excluded from auth file.
  – PA Required (Out of Scope): flagged for OOS Medical Notification.
• Reviewer proceeds only with In Scope components.

data.title: LEVEL 1 (A1 → A2 intake) Delegated Reconsideration / Appeal

data.content:
• CRS locates denied case in MATIS.
• Appeal CTA used to create record linked to original denial.
• TAT timer starts when AOR received (not at logging).
• AOR mailed / uploaded manually by CRS.

• Timeliness and AOR validated.
• Delegation and appealable criteria confirmed.
• Assigned to a reviewer different from the original denial reviewer (per separation of functions).
• 30-day standard TAT clock starts
• “All information received?” → No → initiate outreach (fax / phone) . Clinical team issues CCF fax or calls provider for clarification.
• Record attempts per policy; review continues pending response..

• Validation failed for any of the following:
  – Missing or invalid AOR (Appointment of Representative).
  – Untimely filing (outside CMS window).
  – Non-appealable issue or out of delegated scope.
• Dismissal Notification faxed/mailed to provider and member.
• Reported to Health Plan and CMS per delegation rules.

• Full regimen-level review performed.
• Decision timestamp recorded.
• Partial approvals avoided via provider withdrawal & resubmission.
• Templates used for Part B / Part D letters.sponse..

data.title: EXCEPTION APPEALS (Parallel to Level 1) Part D Formulary / Step Therapy / QLE

data.content:
• CRS receives a provider-initiated exception request for a Part D drug.
• Request type captured as Formulary Exception, Step Therapy Exception, or Quantity Limit (QL) Exception.
• Linked to the original denied or restricted authorization.
• AOR validation not required if provider is prescribing clinician.
• TAT and priority assigned (standard = 72 hr expedited / 30 day standard).

• Confirm drug, regimen, and policy type (Formulary / Step Therapy / QL).
• Verify plan rules allow delegated exception review.
• Confirm documentation includes medical rationale and previous therapy history.
• If incomplete → initiate provider outreach before routing to clinical.
• Clinician issues Clinical Consultation Fax (CCF) or places phone outreach.
• Common examples: missing prior therapy record, missing rationale for exceeding QL, or insufficient documentation for non-formulary request.
• Case remains open until info received or until outreach timeframe expires.

• Formulary Exception Approval: drug not on formulary, but justification shows it’s medically appropriate (e.g., intolerance / contraindication to formulary drugs).
• Step Therapy Exception Approval: member previously failed or could not tolerate prerequisite therapy.
• Quantity Limit Exception Approval: clinician demonstrates higher quantity needed for safe or effective therapy (supported by NCCN / peer-reviewed evidence).
• Clinical rationale and references documented; letter templates generated for provider + member (Part D).

• CRS creates new A2 case referencing A1.
• Status = Pending IRE decision.
• Capture package creation date & contact details.

• Log contact entry (Call Type = “IRE Package Sent”).
• Upload documents (transmittal form, case summary, letters).

• CRS updates A2 status with IRE decision (Upheld / Overturned).
• If Overturned → update effectuation and notify plan / CMS.

• CRS creates A3 record for tracking only.
• Await ALJ determination via plan notification (Part B) or directly (Part D)..

• CRS updates A3 case with final ALJ decision (Upheld / Overturned).
• No further clinical activity within OH.
EXCEPTION APPEALS (Parallel to Level 1) Part D Formulary / Step Therapy / QLE

From Neal Ruggles:

When standing up the services, we have this package to abstract a lot of the minutia of setting up a service. It'd be awesome if you can re-use it to keep all new infrastructure consistent!

 

https://dev.azure.com/oncologyanalytics/Technology/_git/oncohealth-um-common-api

 

Consuming it just involves defining a ServiceDefinition in the root of the API proj:

https://dev.azure.com/oncologyanalytics/Technology/_git/oncohealth-um-api-notification-service?path…

 

and in Program.cs, you extend BaseService:

https://dev.azure.com/oncologyanalytics/Technology/_git/oncohealth-um-api-notification-service?path…

 

you get DI registration, Okta AuthN/AuthZ, KeyVault, Redis, FeatureFlags (via env configs), and Swagger for free

 

For DI, you just define a Bootstrapper file at each layer (API, Business, Client, Data, etc.) and the startup automatically registers everything:

https://dev.azure.com/oncologyanalytics/Technology/_git/oncohealth-um-api-notification-service?path…

New concept for case storage:

  1. Most case state in per-case SQLite files stored as blobs, owned entirely by Case CRUD component. Records are write-only.
  2. Case SQLite includes copies of write-only records from other schemas. If one of those updates outside the event, create an event to update the case and fire rules. Case engine will never modify these records; other schemas are source-of-truth.
  3. Most schema migrations can be handled lazily by writing C# functions to set values for new fields (never alter old fields)
  4. Case Meta Schema is not write-only and contains:
  5. path to SQLite blob
  6. case ID
  7. member ID
  8. payer ID
  9. provider IDs
  10. episode ID?
  11. drug IDs
  12. lock for SQLite blob? (or use Redis)
  13. Case Meta Schema also contains a write-only table relating case IDs to update metadata (config version, etc)
  14. The Case CRUD layer can merge SQLite files for cases in the same episode.


Advantages:

  1. case store is fully scalable in volume and accesses
  2. case data can't be split up by accident
  3. hot/cold storage can be handled at infrastructure layer
  4. core data model encapsulated

Drawbacks:

  1. to reverse a bad configuration, must open each SQLite file
  2. need custom migration logic
  3. need to maintain bulk ingest APIs
Agile Teams - Establish MS Team, Establish Daily Standup, SharePoint Folder?,
Good use of AI to automatically parse/sort doc, but that's out of scope for now.

SOW uses "Working Team Meetings" 

weekly, team leads

Should the Queue Manager touch the Case itself?
e.g. modify priority? Maybe. It might be best to treat the queuing system as a kind of control plane for a case. If priority changes, use Case CRUD.
